title: Alpha phylogenetic diversity
text: ps_diversity() function calculates following alpha diversity measures. wide variety phylogentically-informed diversity metrics literature, phylospatial package focuses primarily “node-based” diversity metrics like Faith’s PD give equal treatment clades/branches levels. Phylogenetic diversity metrics can categorized addressing richness, divergence, regularity (Tucker et al. 2017), listed . Note alternative approach assessing divergence null model analysis richness metrics, discussed later vignette. Richness metrics: PD: Phylogenetic diversity, .e. total length branch segments present location PE: Phylogenetic endemism, .e. endemism-weighted PD ShPD: Shannon’s pylogenetic diversity, .k.“phylogenetic entropy” ShPE: Shannon’s pylogenetic diversity, weighted endemism SiPD: Simpson’s phylogenetic diversity SiPE: Simpson’s phylogenetic diversity, weighted endemism TR: Terminal richness, .e. richness terminal taxa (many cases species). binary data total number terminals site, quantitative data sum probability abundance values. TE: Terminal endemism, .e. total endemism-weighted diversity terminal taxa (.k.. “weighted endemism”) CR: Clade richness, .e. richness taxa levels (equivalent PD cladogram) CE: Clade endemism, .e. total endemism-weighted diversity taxa levels (equivalent PE cladrogram) Divergence metrics: RPD: Relative phylogenetic diversity, .e. mean branch segment length residents (equivalent PD / CR) RPE: Relative phylogenetic endemism, .e. mean endemism-weighted branch length (equivalent PE / CE) MPDT: Mean pairwise distance terminals, .e. classic MPD measure MPDN: Mean pairwise distance nodes, node-based version MPD calculates average branch length separating pairs collateral (non-lineal) nodes including terminals internal nodes, giving representation deeper branches Regularity metrics: VPDT: Variance pairwise distances terminals VPDN: Variance pairwise distances collateral nodes measures use quantitative community data provided. “Endemism” inverse total occurrence mass (sum presence, probability, abundance values) across sites analysis. See ?ps_diversity equations giving derivation metric. Let’s compute diversity metrics phylospatial data set. Since data raster-based, default function return SpatRaster layer measure. ’ll make plots PD PE:
code: div <- ps_diversity(ps, metric = c("PD", "PE")) tm_shape(div$PD) +        tm_raster(col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "inferno")) +       tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE) tm_shape(div$PE) +        tm_raster(col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "inferno")) +       tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

(wanted identify “statistically significant” grid cells one-tailed test alpha = 0.05, cells values greater 0.95.)  numerous alternative options randomization algorithms, choice depend type occurrence data (probability, binary, abundance) attributes terminal community matrix (fill, row column sums, etc.) want hold fixed. addition "quantize" function used , include “nullcat" randomizations binary data, basic "tip_shuffle" randomization (default algorithm), range algorithms defined vegan package "nullmodel" function, option supply custom randomization function. second example, ’s randomization abundance data set, using "abuswap_c" algorithm provided vegan::nullmodel (note since ps_suggest_n_iter() doesn’t work vegan, need set n_iter arbitrarily):

title: Alpha phylogenetic diversity
text: Standard null models treat sites exchangeable, meaning species can shuffled pair sites regardless far apart . may ecologically realistic: species likely co-occur nearby sites distant ones. Spatially constrained null models address weighting randomization exchanges occur frequently nearby sites. Weights can continuous binary can reflect geographic proximity, environmental similarity, presence discrete geographic regions, spatial properties. Weighting available fun = "quantize" (quantitative data) fun = "nullcat" (binary data), fun = "vegan" methods. eligible methods, wt_row argument ps_rand() accepts square weight matrix controlling pairs sites likely exchange species randomization. natural choice distance-decay function geographic distance, can computed using ps_geodist(); choice decay function bandwidth affect strength spatial constraint, based spatial scale ecological processes study system. code demonstrates weight matrix construction common shapes decay function (Gaussian, exponential, threshold), also scheme involving isolated subregions (e.g. islands) never exchange species. Note adding weights generally increases value n_iter needed convergence.
code: # Weight matrices for several alternative distance functions geo <- as.matrix(ps_geodist(ps_bin)) / 1000 # distance in km W <- dnorm(geo, sd = 100) # Gaussian decay w0th 100 km SD W <- exp(-geo / median(geo)) # exponential kernel W <- (geo < 200) + 0 #  hard distance threshold  # Weights matrix for discrete isolated regions island <- sample(1:5, nrow(ps$comm), replace = T) W <- (as.matrix(dist(island)) == 0) + 0  # Spatially constrained randomization iters <- ps_suggest_n_iter(ps_bin, fun = "nullcat", method = "curvecat", n_iter = 3e5) rand_spatial <- ps_rand(ps_bin, fun = "nullcat", method = "curvecat", n_iter = iters,                          n_rand = 1000, metric = c("PD", "PE"),                         wt_row = W)

title: Phylogenetic beta diversity
text: phylospatial package provides range methods calculating pairwise community phylogenetic distances among locations. can calculate phylogenetic versions quantitative community dissimilarity metric available trough vegan package, including various predefined indices provided vegan::vegdist well custom indices specified vegan::designdist. default metric Bray-Curtis distance, also known quantitative Sorensen’s dissimilarity. Additional choices allow partitioning dissimilarity turnover nestedness components. Dissimilarity computed using function ps_add_dissim(), adds distance matrix dissim slot phylospatial data set. just want matrix , can use ps_dissim(). addition specifying dissimilarity index use, functions include options different ways scale phylogenetic community matrix calculating dissimilarity. Setting endemism = TRUE scale every lineage’s occurrence values sum 1 across sites, giving greater weight narrowly distributed taxa. Setting normalize = TRUE scales every site’s total occurrence value sum 1 across taxa, results distance matrix emphasizes proportional differences composition rather alpha diversity gradients. Let’s run example using quantitative Sorensen’s index, weighted endemism. Printing result, can see now contains dissimilarity data; square distance matrix row column site:
code: ps <- ps_add_dissim(ps, method = "sorensen", endemism = TRUE, normalize = TRUE) ps #> `phylospatial` object #>   - 884 lineages across 527 occupied sites (1116 total)  #>   - community data type: probability  #>   - spatial data class: SpatRaster  #>   - dissimilarity data: sorensen

title: Phylogenetic beta diversity
text: One frequent use dissimilarity matrices analyze commmunity phylogentic similarity declines geographic distance, environmental difference, geographic properties. Common methods kind analysis include generalized dissimilarity modeling partial Mantel tests. let’s just visualize phylogenetic turnover changes distance sites. can compute pairwise geographic distance matrix using ps_geodist(). Let’s also compare non-phylogenetic species turnover, can compute specifying tips_only = TRUE. can summarize pattern LOESS curve:
code: phy_beta <- as.vector(ps_dissim(ps, method = "sorensen")) spp_beta <- as.vector(ps_dissim(ps, method = "sorensen", tips_only = TRUE)) geo_dist <- as.vector(ps_geodist(ps)) / 1000  # convert to km  # subsample for plotting (full pairwise set is huge) ss <- sample(length(phy_beta), 5000)  plot(geo_dist[ss], phy_beta[ss],      pch = ".", col = adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.3),      xlab = "Geographic distance (km)",      ylab = "Dissimilarity",      ylim = c(0, 1)) points(geo_dist[ss], spp_beta[ss],        pch = ".", col = adjustcolor("tomato", 0.3))   # loess trend lines lo_phy <- loess(phy_beta[ss] ~ geo_dist[ss]) lo_spp <- loess(spp_beta[ss] ~ geo_dist[ss]) ox <- order(geo_dist[ss]) lines(geo_dist[ss][ox], predict(lo_phy)[ox], col = "steelblue", lwd = 2) lines(geo_dist[ss][ox], predict(lo_spp)[ox], col = "tomato", lwd = 2)   legend("bottomright",        legend = c("Phylogenetic", "Species"),        col = c("steelblue", "tomato"),        lwd = 2, bty = "n")

title: Spatial phylogentic data
text: Let’s begin creating simple phylospatial object. , use phylospatial() function, two key arguments: tree, phylogeny class phylo, comm, community data set representing geographic distributions terminal taxa (usually species). code , simulate random tree five terminal taxa, raster data set 100 grid cells containing occurrence probabilities terminal, layer names corresponding species tree. (differentiating feature phylospatial library supports quantitative data types like probabilities abundances, addition binary community data.) pass phylospatial():
code: library(phylospatial); library(terra); library(ape); library(sf)  # simulate data set.seed(1234) n_taxa <- 5 x <- y <- 10 tree <- rtree(n_taxa) comm <- rast(array((sin(seq(0, pi*12, length.out = n_taxa * x * y)) + 1)/2,                     dim = c(x, y, n_taxa))) names(comm) <- tree$tip.label  # create phylospatial object ps <- phylospatial(comm, tree) ps #> `phylospatial` object #>   - 8 lineages across 100 occupied sites (100 total)  #>   - community data type: probability  #>   - spatial data class: SpatRaster  #>   - dissimilarity data: none

title: Conservation prioritization
text: Phylogenetic diversity originally conceived metric inform conservation, spatial conservation planning core application spatial phylogenetics. Spatial conservation planning involves identifying priority locations actions like creation new protected areas. diversity sophisticated tools available conservation planning. package performs conservation prioritization using stepwise algorithm selects ordered ranking priority sites creation new protected areas. Unlike many algorithms, method can utilize quantitative community data (rather just binary presence-absence data), can utilize quantitative data relative degree protection offered different types protected area (rather just binary protected-unprotected data). Prioritization handled function ps_prioritize(). site’s priority ranking function : current protection level occurrence quantities lineages present site, including terminal taxa larger clades evolutionary branch lengths lineages phylogeny, represent unique evolutionary heritage impact protecting site lineages’ range-wide protection levels compositional complementarity site, high-priority sites, existing protected areas relative cost protecting site lambda, free parameter determining shape conservation benefit function (see ) vignette covers basic conservation optimization, probabilistic prioritization, detail conservation benefit functions.

title: Conservation prioritization
text: Let’s use example data set California mosses perform conservation optimization ranks every grid cell across state. every step iterative ps_prioritize() algorithm, marginal value fully protecting individual site calculated. basic optimization (method = "optimal"), site highest marginal value marked protected, marginal values re-calculated, process repeated sites (max_iter sites) protected. Sites selected earlier process considered higher priority. addition required spatial phylogenetic data set, two optional data inputs one might want provide. first init, location effectiveness existing protected areas. can binary data representing protected versus unprotected sites, continuous data values 0 1 representing degree protection (example, perhaps given spatial unit partially covered protected land, land management sites like national forests partly oriented toward biodiversity protection). conservation optimization, protection level newly protected sites raised 1 (alternative level specified parameter level), greater benefit resulting sites lower initial values. second optional variable cost, representing relative cost protecting different sites across study area. Sites high benefit--cost ratios prioritized. example, ’ll just specify arbitrary initial protection gradient north south cost gradient east west, though course real analysis require actual data. simplicity ’ll provide vectors (length equal ps$n_sites, total number grid cells including unoccupied ones), also provide raster layers matching spatial element data set. First let’s load libraries ’ll need, initialize phylospatial data set using example data California mosses. (See vignette("phylospatial-data") details constructing phylospatial objects.) ’ll also create variable called init specifying (arbitrary) initial conservation values, cost defining hypothetical land cost data. Now ’ll pass inputs ps_prioritize(). Plotting result, highest-priority sites low rank values, shown yellow.
code: library(phylospatial); library(tmap); library(magrittr)  ps <- moss() init <- seq(1, 0, length.out = ps$n_sites) cost <- runif(ps$n_sites, 10, 1000) priority <- ps_prioritize(ps, init = init, cost = cost)  tm_shape(priority) +        tm_raster(col.scale = tm_scale_continuous_log(values = "-inferno")) +        tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

title: Conservation prioritization
text: routine shown gives optimal priority ranking, based assumption sites selected optimal order. informative result relatively lightweight computation, limitations. First, assuming optimal behavior may unrealistic. second, since algorithm values complementarity (.e. protection sites distinct, non-redundant biotic communities), sites high conservation value attractive real-world priorities can entirely overlooked compositionally similar sites already selected slightly higher value. Probabilistic prioritization, activated method = "probable", addresses issues. iteration, instead protecting site highest marginal value done "optimal" method, approach protects random site, selected probability function site’s marginal value. trade-individual runs algorithm can highly variable, algorithm needs run many times, prioritization rankings summarized across repeated runs. using probabilistic method, ps_prioritize() returns summary statistics site including average priority rank across reps, various quantiles rank distribution, proportion reps site among top-ranked sites. Running large number n_reps can substantially increase computation times, two ways help mitigate run times. First, can use parallel processing increasing n_cores default 1. Second, can set max_iter relatively small number, stops algorithm number sites added. example, data set 1000 sites, setting max_iter = 10 can reduce run times almost two orders magnitude. won’t get full rank prioritization sites individual rep, get proportion reps site top 10, arguably even useful. Let’s demonstrate ; ’ll run 2500 reps, though might better real analysis:
code: priority <- ps_prioritize(ps, init = init, cost = cost, n_reps = 2500,                           method = "prob", max_iter = 10)  tm_shape(priority$top10) +        tm_raster(col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "inferno"),                 col.legend = tm_legend(title = "proporiton of runs\nin which site was\ntop-10 priority")) +        tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

title: Spatial Phylogenetic Analysis
text: phylospatial R package spatial phylogenetic diversity analysis—accounting evolutionary relationships among taxa describing biodiversity patterns. package provides functions building analyzing phylospatial data: phylospatial() constructs spatial phylogenetic data set community data tree. ps_diversity() calculates range phylogenetic diversity endemism metrics. ps_rand() computes significance values diversity metrics using null model randomizations. ps_dissim() calculates pairwise community phylogenetic beta diversity matrix. ps_ordinate() performs community ordination reduce dimensionality data set. ps_regions() clusters sites phylogenetically similar biogeographic regions. ps_prioritize() performs spatial optimization identify conservation priorities. key feature phylospatial full support quantitative community data, including occurrence probabilities (e.g., species distribution models) abundances, addition binary presence-absence data. avoids information loss thresholding continuous data, enables new analyses available spatial phylogenetic R packages.

title: Create a spatial phylogenetic object — phylospatial
text: phylospatial object, list containing following elements: "data_type": Character indicating community data type "tree": Phylogeny class phylo "comm": Community matrix containing occupied sites, including column every terminal taxon every larger clade. Column order corresponds tree edge order. "spatial": SpatRaster sf providing spatial coordinates sites (including unoccupied). May missing spatial data supplied. "occupied": Integer vector row indices identifying sites original data occupied. "n_sites": Total number sites original data, including unoccupied. "dissim": community dissimilarity matrix class dist indicating pairwise phylogenetic dissimilarity occupied sites. Missing unless ps_add_dissim() called.

title: Create a spatial phylogenetic object — phylospatial

title: Categorical Analysis of Neo- and Paleo-Endemism (CANAPE) — ps_canape
text: Endemism significance categories defined follows: Endemism significant: neither PE CE significantly high alpha. Significant neoendemism: PE CE significantly high alpha; RPE significantly low alpha / 2 (two-tailed test). Significant paleoendemism: PE CE significantly high alpha; RPE significantly high alpha / 2 (two-tailed test).. Significant mixed-endemism: PE CE significantly high alpha; RPE significant. Significant super-endemism: PE CE significantly high alpha / 5; RPE significant.

title: Categorical Analysis of Neo- and Paleo-Endemism (CANAPE) — ps_canape
text: Mishler, B. D., Knerr, N., González-Orozco, C. E., Thornhill, . H., Laffan, S. W., & Miller, J. T. (2014). Phylogenetic measures biodiversity neo-paleo-endemism Australian Acacia. Nature Communications, 5(1), 4473.

title: Categorical Analysis of Neo- and Paleo-Endemism (CANAPE) — ps_canape
code: # \donttest{ # classic CANAPE using binary data and the curveball algorithm # (note that a real analysis would require a much higher `n_rand`) set.seed(123456) ps <- ps_simulate(data_type = "binary") rand <- ps_rand(ps, metric = c("PE", "RPE", "CE"),                 fun = "nullmodel", method = "curveball",                 n_rand = 25, burnin = 10000, progress = FALSE) #> Warning: `burnin` is set via `n_iter`; ignoring `burnin` in `...`. canape <- ps_canape(rand) terra::plot(canape)  # }

title: Calculate spatial phylogenetic diversity and endemism metrics — ps_diversity
text: function calculates following metrics. Endemism-weighted versions metrics available. metrics weighted occurrence probability abundance, applicable. Richness measures: TD—Terminal Diversity, .e. richness terminal taxa (many cases species): \(\sum_{t}{p_t}\) TE—Terminal Endemism, .e. total endemism-weighted diversity terminal taxa, .k.. "weighted endemism": \(\sum_{t}{p_t r_t^{-1}}\) CD—Clade Diversity, .e. richness taxa levels (equivalent PD cladogram): \(\sum_{b}{p_b}\) CE—Clade Endemism, .e. total endemism-weighted diversity taxa levels (equivalent PE cladrogram): \(\sum_{b}{p_b r_b^{-1}}\) PD—Phylogenetic Diversity, .e. total branch length occurring site: \(\sum_{b}{L_b p_b}\) PE—Phylogenetic Endemism, .e. endemism-weighted PD: \(\sum_{b}{L_b p_b r_b^{-1}}\) ShPD—Shannon Phylogenetic Diversity, .k.. "phylogenetic entropy" (version log "effective diversity" version based Hill numbers): \(-\sum_{b}{L_b n_b log(n_b)}\) ShPE—Shannon phylogenetic Endemism, endemism-weighted version ShPD: \(-\sum_{b}{L_b n_b log(e_b) r_b^{-1}}\) SiPD—Simpson Phylogenetic Diversity: \(1 / \sum_{b}{L_b n_b^2}\) SiPE—Simpson Phylogenetic Endemism, endemism-weighted version SiPD: \(1 / \sum_{b}{L_b r_b^{-1} e_b^2}\) Divergence measures: RPD—Relative Phylogenetic Diversity, .e. mean branch segment length (equivalent PD / CR): \(\sum_{b}{L_b p_b} / \sum_{b}{p_b}\) RPE—Relative Phylogenetic Endemism, .e. mean endemism-weighted branch segment length (equivalent PE / CE): \(\sum_{b}{L_b p_b r_b^{-1}} / \sum_{b}{p_b r_b^{-1}}\) MPDT—Mean Pairwise Distance Terminals, .e. classic MPD metric. average cophenetic distances, weighted \(p_t\). MPDN—Mean Pairwise Distance Nodes, experimental version MPD considers distances every pair non-nested clades, putting weight deeper branches MPDT. mean distances collateral (non-lineal) node pairs including terminal internal nodes, weighted \(p_b\). Note divergence can also assessed using ps_rand() run null model analyses richness measures like PD. Regularity measures: VPDT—Variance Pairwise Distances Terminals, .e. classic VPD metric, weighted \(p_t\). VPDN—Variance Pairwise Distances Nodes, .e. MPDN variance. equations, \(b\) indexes taxa including terminals larger clades; \(t\) indexes terminals ; \(p_i\) occurrence value (binary, probability, abundance) clade/terminal \(\) given community; \(L_b\) length phylogenetic branch segment unique clade \(b\); \(r_i\) sum \(p_i\) across sites. Shannon Simpson indices, nonzero elements \(p_b\) used, \(n_b = p_b / \sum_{b}{p_b L_b}\), \(e_b = p_b / \sum_{b}{p_b L_b r_b^{-1}}\).

Every time new site protected algorithm progresses, changes marginal conservation value sites. Marginal value increase conservation benefit arise fully protecting given site, divided cost protecting site. calculated function site's current protection level, quantitative presence probability abundance terminal taxa larger clades present site, evolutionary branch lengths phylogeny, impact protecting site range-wide protection levels, free parameter lambda. lambda determines relative importance protecting small portion every taxon's range, versus fully protecting ranges valuable taxa (longer evolutionary branches smaller geographic ranges).

text: New function ps_grid() converts point occurrence data (e.g. GBIF records) raster format suitable use phylospatial functions. New functions ps_suggest_n_iter() ps_trace() provide convergence diagnostics null model randomizations, wrapping nullcat::suggest_n_iter() nullcat::trace_cat() occupied-site tip community matrix. ps_rand() ps_quantize() now expose wt_row wt_col named parameters spatially functionally constrained null models. accept weight matrices (e.g., geographic distance decay matrix ps_geodist()) bias pairs sites species exchange values randomization. ps_rand() gains new fun = "nullcat" path binary data. recommended path binary data convergence diagnostics spatial weights desired. ps_rand() now exposes n_iter named parameter controlling number swap iterations per null matrix. routed nullcat::nullcat(), nullcat::quantize_prep(), vegan::simulate.nullmodel() (burnin) depending selected fun. default 1000 fixes pre-existing issue vegan sequential path used 1 iteration per matrix default.

title: phylospatial 1.3.0
text: ps_dissim() now computes distances much faster via parallelDist relevant metrics, falling back vegan needed. also adds support traditional non-phylogenetic species turnover metrics via new tips_only option. New helper function ps_geodist() computes pairwise geographic distances sites. community matrix (ps$comm) now stores occupied sites, improving speed memory usage datasets many unoccupied cells. Speedups proportional fraction empty sites affect major functions, ps_dissim() seeing largest gains (~4x 50% unoccupied cells) due quadratic scaling. New fields ps$occupied ps$n_sites track rows original data occupied total site count, respectively. New exported function ps_expand() expands occupied-results back full spatial extent NA unoccupied sites.

title: phylospatial 1.3.0
text: nrow(ps$comm) now equals number occupied sites, total cells. Use ps$n_sites total. ps$dissim now dimensioned occupied sites . to_spatial(ps$comm, ps$spatial) longer works directly. Use ps_expand(ps, ps$comm, spatial = TRUE) ps_get_comm(ps) instead. ps_get_comm() spatial = FALSE returns occupied-matrix.

text: CRAN release: 2025-12-20 CRAN compliance: fixed vignette builds conditionally load suggested package ‘tmap’. ps_diversity() now computes smaller set metrics default, order reduce default run times. ps_rand() includes new choice summary statistic: addition default “quantile” function, new “z-score” option available. quantize() ps_rand() now use nullcat::quantize() internally, addressing flaw earlier implementation. phylospatial() functions call now use compute-optimized internal range constructor.
